name: Futakuchi-onna
description: The Futakuchi-onna, or 'two-mouthed woman,' is a Japanese yōkai (supernatural creature) characterized by having a second mouth on the back of her head, beneath her hair. This second mouth often has a mind of its own, demanding food and uttering spiteful remarks. The origin stories of Futakuchi-onna vary, but often involve a woman who was either neglectful of her stepchild or was naturally a miser who rarely ate, leading to the development of the second mouth as a form of supernatural punishment or manifestation of hidden hunger. The second mouth may appear after the woman's head is struck by an object, or it may develop spontaneously. It's a gruesome and unsettling image, reflecting themes of greed, neglect, and the hidden aspects of human nature. The Futakuchi-onna serves as a cautionary tale, reminding listeners of the importance of compassion, generosity, and balance. She is often depicted with long hair that acts as tentacles, feeding the second mouth when the woman tries to ignore it. The creature is a powerful symbol of inner conflict and the consequences of unchecked desires. Her plight is both terrifying and pitiable, illustrating the burdens of repressed emotions and the monstrous forms they can take.
subtitle: The Two-Mouthed Woman of Japanese Folklore
abilities: Insatiable hunger of the second mouth
Independent speech from the second mouth
Hair manipulation for feeding the second mouth
Causing misfortune to those around her
appearance: The Futakuchi-onna appears as a normal woman, but with a second, fully functional mouth on the back of her head, usually hidden beneath her hair. This second mouth has lips, teeth, and a tongue, and is capable of eating and speaking. Her hair often acts as tentacles, reaching for food to feed the mouth when the woman tries to ignore it. The second mouth is often depicted as being larger and more grotesque than a normal mouth.
habitat: Human settlements, particularly households where she lives as a wife or servant.
behavior: The Futakuchi-onna is driven by the insatiable hunger of her second mouth, which demands constant feeding. She may try to hide the mouth from others, but its constant demands and spiteful comments often betray her. She is often portrayed as being unhappy and burdened by the presence of the second mouth, and may lash out at those around her.
weaknesses: Satisfying the hunger of the second mouth (temporarily)

keyMyths.title: The Miserly Wife
keyMyths.description: A stingy woman who barely ate herself developed a second mouth after neglecting her stepchild, symbolizing her repressed hunger and selfishness. The second mouth constantly demands food, tormenting her.

sources.author: Michael Dylan Foster
sources.work: The Book of Yōkai: Mysterious Creatures of Japanese Folklore
sources.description: Provides detailed information on various yōkai, including the Futakuchi-onna.
sources.text: Provides detailed information on various yōkai, including the Futakuchi-onna.
